turns.prompt: make a 10 question quiz on diffusion, actually just 6, no wait make it 10 but only 6 about ddpm
turns.text:
I'll clarify quickly — you want **10 questions total, with 6 focused on DDPM** and 4 on broader diffusion topics (score-based, flow matching, etc.). That's the most useful interpretation.

Ready to fine-tune on the card — adjust difficulty, split, or focus as needed.

params.subtopic: DDPM forward/reverse process, noise schedules, training loss, DDIM sampling, score-based models, flow matching
params.additional_instructions: 6 questions specifically on DDPM (cheat sheet content): forward process, reverse posterior, training objectives, sampling (standard DDPM vs. DDIM), variance selection. 4 questions on broader diffusion topics covered in course materials. Ground in 06_diffusion_ddpm_ddim_notes.pdf for DDPM content.

artifact.title: AI623 Deep Vision Language Models – DDPM & Diffusion Models Assessment

questions.text: In the DDPM forward process, the closed-form expression for jumping directly from a clean image \(X_0\) to a noisy image \(X_t\) is given by \(X_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} X_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \epsilon\), where \(\epsilon \sim N(0, I)\). Which of the following correctly identifies what \(\bar{\alpha}_t\) represents?

The true posterior variance in DDPM is given by \(\tilde{\beta}_t = \frac{\beta_t (1 - \bar{\alpha}_{t-1})}{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\). This term appears in the reverse process formula. What does this posterior variance condition on?

In the DDPM framework, the simplified training loss (L_simple) is \(||\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta||_2^2\), where \(\epsilon\) is the true noise vector and \(\epsilon_\theta\) is the model's prediction. How does this objective relate to the true ELBO loss \(L_{vlb}\)?

During standard DDPM sampling, the denoising step at timestep t is: \(X_{t-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \left( X_t - \frac{\beta_t}{\sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}} \epsilon_\theta \right) + \sigma_t z_t\), where \(z_t \sim N(0, I)\). If the timestep is the final step (t = 1), what should happen to the stochastic term \(\sigma_t z_t\)?

The Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) at timestep t is defined as \(SNR_t = \frac{\bar{\alpha}_t}{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\). As t increases from 0 to T, what happens to the SNR, and what does this tell you about the relative information content in the diffusion process?

State the formula for the true posterior mean \(\tilde{\mu}_t\) conditioned on both the original clean image \(X_0\) and the current noisy image \(X_t\). Then, briefly explain why the model can use a predicted noise \(\epsilon_\theta\) to estimate this mean without access to the true \(X_0\) during sampling.

In standard DDPM, the variance \(\sigma_t^2\) is typically fixed to one of two choices during sampling. Name and define both choices, and explain why fixing the variance (rather than learning it) simplifies the implementation.

DDIM allows non-Markovian jumps from timestep t to an earlier timestep t′ (where t′ < t). The DDIM step predicts an intermediate clean image estimate \(\hat{X}_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}} (X_t - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \epsilon_\theta)\) and then jumps forward with controlled stochasticity. When would you set the stochasticity parameter \(\eta = 0\), and what is the computational consequ

In Improved DDPM, the variance is learned using interpolation: \(\Sigma_\theta = \exp(v \log \beta_t + (1 - v) \log \tilde{\beta}_t)\), where v is a learned scalar between 0 and 1. This formula interpolates between two extremes. What do these two extremes represent, and what is the pedagogical advantage of parameterizing the variance this way rather than learning it directly?

State the reparameterization trick as it applies to the closed-form forward jump in DDPM. Specifically, explain why the sum of multiple independent Gaussian random variables can be expressed as a single Gaussian with combined variance, and how this justifies writing \(X_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} X_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \epsilon\) in closed form.

answer_key.answer: The true posterior mean conditioned on both X_0 and X_t is: μ̃_t = (√ᾱ_{t-1} β_t / (1 - ᾱ_t)) X_0 + (√α_t (1 - ᾱ_{t-1}) / (1 - ᾱ_t)) X_t. Alternatively, this can be rewritten using predicted noise as: μ̃_t = (1/√α_t) (X_t - (β_t / √(1 - ᾱ_t)) ε̂_θ). During sampling, the model never sees X_0; instead

The two standard choices are: (1) β_t (the forward process variance schedule), and (2) β̃_t = β_t (1 - ᾱ_{t-1}) / (1 - ᾱ_t) (the true posterior variance). Fixing the variance rather than learning it simplifies the implementation because the model only needs to predict the noise ε_θ; it does not requ

The reparameterization trick states that the sum of independent Gaussian random variables is also Gaussian, with combined variance. If X = N(μ_1, σ_1^2) and Y = N(μ_2, σ_2^2) are independent Gaussians, then X + Y = N(μ_1 + μ_2, σ_1^2 + σ_2^2). In DDPM's iterative forward process, applying noise repe

turns.prompt: yes 10 total, 6 on ddpm and the other 4 on ddim
turns.text:
Perfect — **10 questions total: 6 on DDPM, 4 on DDIM**, grounded in your attached notes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["10-Question Diffusion Quiz"] --> B["6 DDPM Questions"]
    A --> C["4 DDIM Questions"]
    B --> B1["Forward process"]
    B --> B2["Reverse & posterior"]
    B --> B3["Training loss"]
    C --> C1["Non-Markovian jumps"]
    C --> C2["Stochasticity & determinism"]
```

Let me propose this now — you can tweak the split, question types, or difficulty on the card:
<function_calls>
<invoke name="generate_assessment">
<parameter name="title">AI623 Diffusion Quiz – DDPM & DDIM Focus</parameter>
<parameter name="description">10-question advanced quiz: 6 questions on DDPM (forward process, posterior mean/variance, training loss, sampling boundary conditions) and 4 on DDIM (non-Markovian jumps, stochasticity parameter η, step-skipping, deterministic generation). Mix of MCQ and short-answer. Grounded in 06_diffusion_ddpm_ddim_notes.pdf.</parameter>
<parameter name="topic">DDPM & DDIM Diffusion Models</parameter>
<parameter name="assessment_type">quiz</parameter>
<parameter name="question_count">10</parameter>
<parameter name="question_types">["mcq", "short_answer"]</parameter>
<parameter name="level">advanced</parameter>
<parameter name="language">en</parameter>
<parameter name="additional_instructions">6 questions on DDPM (forward/reverse process, posterior, training loss, sampling); 4 on DDIM (jumps, η parameter, step-skipping, deterministic generation). Reference 06_diffusion_ddpm_ddim_notes.pdf formulas and exam keywords. Include boundary conditions (z=0 at t=1) and the reparameterization trick where relevant.</parameter>
</invoke>
</function_calls>

Done — the quiz is now on the card. Review it, adjust the split or add emphasis on any subtopic, then hit **Generate**.

<function_calls>
<invoke name="refine_assessment">
<parameter name="instruction">Add a complete answer key to this quiz. For each of the 10 questions (6 DDPM + 4 DDIM), provide: (a) the correct answer or sample solution for MCQs and short-answers respectively, (b) brief pedagogical explanation (1-2 sentences) of why that answer is correct, referencing the key formulas from 06_diffusion_ddpm_ddim_notes.pdf where relevant (e.g. closed-form forward jump, posterior variance formula, DDIM step with η parameter). Make the answer key detailed enough for a teacher to grade and give feedback, but concise enough to fit cleanly on one or two pages.</parameter>

</invoke>

Done — adding the answer key with explanations for all 10 questions now. You'll see the full key on the updated card, ready to grade with.
